title: Optical Scale Up Fabrics Are Limited By Manufacturing, Not Architecture
gist: A Scintil Photonics CEO argues the AI scale-up optics debate is settled at the architecture level (slow-and-wide NRZ DWDM per the new OCI MSA), and the real bottleneck to reaching 1.6 Tb/sec fibers is manufacturing: only heterogeneous integration of III-V lasers onto silicon photonics wafers can produce precision laser arrays at the millions-per-month hyperscale volumes CPO requires.

insights.claim: AMD, Broadcom, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Nvidia, and OpenAI formed the Optical Compute Interconnect MSA (OCI MSA) this spring, standardizing co-packaged-optics scale-up networks on slow-and-wide NRZ modulation with wavelength-division multiplexing.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: A six-hyperscaler consortium settling on one CPO architecture concentrates the addressable market for optics suppliers who fit that spec.
insights.quote: This consortium settled the architectural debate over the direction of networking in AI.

insights.claim: OCI GEN1 specifies four wavelengths at 50 Gb/sec NRZ (200 Gb/sec per fiber per direction, 400 GHz channel spacing) as a starting point, with a roadmap scaling to 1.6 Tb/sec per fiber per direction.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: GEN1 is explicitly a minimum, not maximum; suppliers whose light-source architecture can't extend to 8/16 wavelengths face a two-year redesign penalty.
insights.quote: OCI GEN1 sets the first minimum multi-wavelength standard, not the maximum.

insights.claim: Slow-and-wide NRZ is chosen for energy per bit: NRZ needs ~3x less optical power than PAM-4 for the same bit-error rate, and SerDes power per bit at 50 GBaud is roughly one-third that at 100 GBaud.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Explains why the industry scales bandwidth by adding wavelengths rather than symbol rate; a durable technical lens for evaluating optics roadmaps.

themes.theme: Wavelength scaling as the new axis

insights.claim: Bandwidth scales with wavelength count on unchanged per-channel electronics: 8 wavelengths deliver 400 Gbps per fiber per direction and 16 deliver 800, so doubling wavelengths doubles bandwidth without doubling design cost.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Wavelength-scaling headroom on the same manufacturing flow becomes a key procurement metric alongside energy/bit and latency.
insights.quote: Doubling the wavelengths doubles the bandwidth without doubling the design cost.

insights.claim: Ayar Labs has pursued the 8-to-16 wavelength path for years, and Nvidia's published roadmap models a 16-wavelength interconnect as the route to its sub-1 pJ/bit energy targets.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Both a key optics startup and Nvidia already commit to high-wavelength counts, signaling 4-wavelength GEN1 is transitional; back suppliers on the 16+ path.
insights.locator: First-movers have already settled this argument

insights.claim: Today's wavelength-count decision sets the ceiling on which AI models a cluster can run in 2028 and beyond, because larger flat low-latency scale-up domains enlarge working memory, context windows, and transformer depth.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Directly links optics interconnect choices to future model capability, so scale-up fabric investment is a leading indicator of frontier-model scaling limits.
insights.quote: Today's wavelength-count decision sets the ceiling on which models the resulting cluster can run in 2028 and beyond.
insights.locator: The bigger payoff is what cluster size unlocks

insights.claim: Hyperscale CPO will need millions of laser source units per month, not tens of thousands, and discrete-laser supply chains cannot reach those volumes, which (not architecture) is what has actually held DWDM back as deployable.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Reframes the optics investment thesis around manufacturing scalability of laser arrays, not architecture; the true supplier moat.
insights.quote: Volume is the binding constraint. Hyperscale CPO will need millions of laser source units per month, not tens of thousands

insights.claim: Both discrete-laser paths fail at scale: shared-laser designs lose optical power to splitting losses that compound past 4 wavelengths, while dedicated-laser designs need ~128 lasers, alignments, and photodiodes for a 16-wavelength/8-fiber module, with failure rates compounding at every micrometer-tolerance interface.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Quantifies why discrete optics can't scale; suppliers still on discrete assembly are structurally capped.

themes.theme: Heterogeneous integration as the CMOS of photonics

insights.claim: Photonic integration has moved through three eras (discrete assembly, silicon photonics that excluded III-V lasers/SOAs/modulators, and now heterogeneous integration of all photonic materials on one wafer), with the third era finally putting lasers on a semiconductor learning curve.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: The three-era framework is a screen for optics vendors: only those in the heterogeneous-integration era inherit CMOS-like cost curves.
insights.locator: Photonic integration has evolved through three manufacturing eras

insights.claim: Scintil's SHIP (Scintil Heterogeneous Integrated Photonics) runs today on Tower Semiconductor's silicon photonics platform on 200mm production lines that already turn out tens of millions of pluggable transceivers, with a 300mm path next.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Scintil claims production-ready volume capability on an existing foundry, its core competitive pitch versus discrete-laser rivals.

insights.claim: At OFC 2026, four architecturally distinct system vendors independently requested SHIP extensions (deeper transceiver integration, optical switching, high-speed modulation, integrated amplification), each needing III-V integration into the silicon photonics flow.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Convergent independent demand for one integration capability is an early customer-traction signal for heterogeneous-integration suppliers.
insights.locator: The platform is already reaching beyond the laser source

insights.claim: Scintil's LEAF Light, a single-chip DWDM-native light engine, has been demonstrated in 8- and 16-wavelength configurations compatible with microring-based CPO transceivers, and Nvidia participated in Scintil's Series B as ecosystem alignment.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: Nvidia's Series B stake in Scintil signals which optics manufacturing approach the dominant GPU vendor is hedging on; a strategic tell for the CPO supply chain.
insights.quote: Scintil's Series B included participation from Nvidia as part of broader ecosystem alignment.
insights.locator: LEAF Light is the production proof

insights.claim: Teams designing for heterogeneous integration now will hold the architectural position when it becomes default, while those deferring the manufacturing question will spend two generations rebuilding programs around suppliers who solved it earlier.
insights.speaker: Matt Crowley
insights.so_what: A vendor-authored but concrete claim of a two-generation switching penalty; useful for judging which optics roadmaps carry lock-in risk. Note author is Scintil's CEO.
insights.quote: The teams that defer the manufacturing question will spend two generations rebuilding programs around suppliers who solved it earlier.
insights.locator: What Comes After The First Generation
